mappings.rationale: The definitive overview, spanning offensive weaponry (A), defensive systems (B), weapon platforms (C), and the engineering, logistics, and electronics (D-F) that support them.
Nuclear fission and fusion weapons (A.5) transformed warfare, and this volume explains their energy sources, blast effects, heat, and radioactive fallout.
Self-propelled missiles (A.4), military aircraft (C.4), and electromagnetic sensors (F.1) define modern warfare, and this volume integrates them into a coherent picture.
Traces the earliest hand-powered weapons—shock weapons, missile weapons, mechanical siege engines (A.1)—and the permanent fortifications built to resist them (B.2.b).
Strategic thinking determines how weapon platforms (C), logistics (E), and military technology are deployed to achieve political objectives.
Military aircraft (C.4)—fighters, bombers, helicopters, and transport planes—reshaped warfare from World War I onward.
Traces the evolution of American weaponry from gunpowder arms (A.2) through guided missiles (A.4) and the platforms (C) that carried them into battle.
Naval vessels evolved from galleons to nuclear aircraft carriers, illuminating surface ships (C.2) and submarines (C.3) as weapon platforms.
World War I introduced chemical weapons (A.6), early tanks (C.1.c-d), and trench fortification (B.2), while artillery and small arms (A.2) reached industrial scale.
World War II accelerated military technology across every domain: aircraft (C.4), naval vessels (C.2.c), nuclear weapons (A.5), and radar (F.1).
The gunpowder era's apex saw artillery and small arms (A.2) reach new effectiveness alongside permanent fortification systems (B.2.c).
The superpower arms race drove ballistic missiles (A.4), nuclear weapons (A.5), and missile defense systems (B.3) to unprecedented levels of sophistication.
Clausewitz's theoretical framework for war informs the strategic application of engineering (D), logistics (E), and military technology at every level.
Signal security and encrypted communications are vital aspects of military electronics (F.1), from Enigma to modern digital encryption.
Nuclear propulsion powers aircraft carriers, cruisers (C.2.e), and submarines (C.3.a), giving naval forces virtually unlimited range.
Autonomous weapons systems and unmanned platforms are reshaping weapon platforms (C) and self-propelled missile technology (A.4).
Explosives, grenades, mines, and bombs (A.3) alongside small arms (A.2.d) are wielded in unconventional warfare contexts that challenge traditional military frameworks.
